frames.caption: Reorder 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 into 1 → 5 → 2 → 4 → 3 in place. It chains three classic sub-routines: find middle, reverse second half, weave.
frames.note: Reorder List is the canonical "compose primitives" linked-list problem. None of the three moves is new — midpoint (fast/slow), reverse (prev/curr), and alternate-merge (two-pointer weave) — but stitching them in the right order, with the right midpoint variant and the right cut, is the whole exercise.

frames.caption: Phase 1: find the middle. slow and fast both start at the head.
frames.note: Phase 1 — find the split point. fast hops two nodes for every one of slow, so when fast can no longer take a full double-step, slow sits at the END of the first half. The guard `fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null` is the FIRST-middle variant: for the even case it leaves the first half no shorter than the second, which is exactly what lets the later weave consume both halves cleanly.

frames.caption: Advance: slow → 2 (one step), fast → 3 (two steps).
frames.note: Phase 1 — find the split point. fast hops two nodes for every one of slow, so when fast can no longer take a full double-step, slow sits at the END of the first half. The guard `fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null` is the FIRST-middle variant: for the even case it leaves the first half no shorter than the second, which is exactly what lets the later weave consume both halves cleanly.

frames.caption: Advance: slow → 3 (one step), fast → 5 (two steps).
frames.note: Phase 1 — find the split point. fast hops two nodes for every one of slow, so when fast can no longer take a full double-step, slow sits at the END of the first half. The guard `fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null` is the FIRST-middle variant: for the even case it leaves the first half no shorter than the second, which is exactly what lets the later weave consume both halves cleanly.

frames.caption: fast can't take another double-step. slow stopped at 3 — the end of the first half (1 → 2 → 3).
frames.note: Phase 1 — find the split point. fast hops two nodes for every one of slow, so when fast can no longer take a full double-step, slow sits at the END of the first half. The guard `fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null` is the FIRST-middle variant: for the even case it leaves the first half no shorter than the second, which is exactly what lets the later weave consume both halves cleanly.

frames.caption: Phase 2: split. second = slow.next = 4 (start of the back half).
frames.note: Phase 2 begins by splitting: `second = slow.next` grabs the start of the back half, then `slow.next = null` severs the list into two independent chains. Cutting here is mandatory — without it the reversed half would still loop back into the front half and you would build a cycle.

frames.caption: Cut: slow.next = null severs the list into two independent chains (1 → 2 → 3 and 4 → 5).
frames.note: Phase 2 begins by splitting: `second = slow.next` grabs the start of the back half, then `slow.next = null` severs the list into two independent chains. Cutting here is mandatory — without it the reversed half would still loop back into the front half and you would build a cycle.

frames.caption: Reverse the back half: flip 4.next back to prev, then advance. Reversed so far ends at 4.
frames.note: Reverse the second half in place — the same prev/curr dance as Reverse Linked List. Save second.next FIRST, flip second.next back to prev, then advance both. When second falls off the end, prev is the head of the reversed back half (5 → 4).

frames.caption: Reverse the back half: flip 5.next back to prev, then advance. Reversed so far ends at 5.
frames.note: Reverse the second half in place — the same prev/curr dance as Reverse Linked List. Save second.next FIRST, flip second.next back to prev, then advance both. When second falls off the end, prev is the head of the reversed back half (5 → 4).

frames.caption: Back half reversed: second = 5 heads it (5 → 4). Two clean chains ready to weave.
frames.note: The back half is now reversed: prev (node 5) is its new head, which we store in `second`. Two clean chains remain — first half 1 → 2 → 3 and reversed back half 5 → 4 — ready to be interleaved.

frames.caption: Phase 3: weave. first = head = 1, second = 5. Interleave first, second, first, second, …
frames.note: Phase 3 — weave alternately. Cache BOTH next-pointers (t1, t2) before overwriting anything, then splice second in right after first and hop both pointers forward one slot. Each pass stitches one back-half node between two front-half nodes; the front half is one longer (or equal), so it owns the final tail and the loop ends with second == null.

frames.caption: Splice 5 in right after 1, then advance both. Woven prefix: 1 → 5 → 2 → 3.
frames.note: Phase 3 — weave alternately. Cache BOTH next-pointers (t1, t2) before overwriting anything, then splice second in right after first and hop both pointers forward one slot. Each pass stitches one back-half node between two front-half nodes; the front half is one longer (or equal), so it owns the final tail and the loop ends with second == null.

frames.caption: Splice 4 in right after 2, then advance both. Woven prefix: 1 → 5 → 2 → 4 → 3.
frames.note: Phase 3 — weave alternately. Cache BOTH next-pointers (t1, t2) before overwriting anything, then splice second in right after first and hop both pointers forward one slot. Each pass stitches one back-half node between two front-half nodes; the front half is one longer (or equal), so it owns the final tail and the loop ends with second == null.

frames.caption: second is null — woven. Result: 1 → 5 → 2 → 4 → 3.
frames.note: second is null — every back-half node has been woven in. The list now reads 1 → 5 → 2 → 4 → 3: original head, original tail, second, second-to-last, and so on. Reorder is just three primitives chained — midpoint, reverse, alternate-merge — each O(n) time and O(1) space, so the whole routine is O(n)/O(1).
